docs.title: Proxmox Lab Infrastructure Training
docs.text: Welcome to the comprehensive 15-day infrastructure training project. This documentation will guide you through building a complete enterprise infrastructure from bare metal to production-ready services.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
This training covers the complete lifecycle of modern infrastructure deployment:

  • Virtualization & Storage: Proxmox VE with ZFS storage pools
  • Identity Management: OpenLDAP directory services with Keycloak SSO
  • Collaboration Tools: Rocket.Chat and Nextcloud deployment
  • Security: CIS benchmark hardening, firewalls, and intrusion prevention
  • Monitoring: Prometheus and Grafana observability stack
  • Documentation: MkDocs for knowledge management

docs.title: Training Timeline
docs.text: Day Topic Duration 1 Rescue mode, ZFS setup, Proxmox installation 4-6 hours 2 Create 6 virtual machines 4-5 hours 3 Rocket.Chat installation 3-4 hours 4 Nextcloud installation 3-4 hours 5 Nginx reverse proxy and SSL 4-6 hours 6 OpenLDAP server setup 3-4 hours 7 Keycloak installation and realm setup 4-5 hours 8 OIDC integration for SSO 3-4 hours 9 CIS hardening 6-8 hours 10 Firewall, Fail2Ban, and backups 4-5 hours 11 Prometheus and Grafana monitoring 4-5 hours 12 MkDocs documentation server 3-4 hours 13 Security audits 4-5 hours 14 Final testing and validation 4-5 hours 15 Advanced topics (optional) Variable

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • Hetzner EX44 server (or equivalent with 2x NVMe drives)
  • Basic Linux command line knowledge
  • SSH client installed
  • Text editor (nano, vim, or VS Code)
  • 15 days of dedicated time (4-6 hours per day)

docs.text:
1. Prerequisites - What you need before Day 1
  2. Rescue Mode - Begin Day 1

docs.title: Infrastructure Building
docs.text:

  2. Software Installation - Deploy services

  2. Monitoring Setup - Observe everything
  3. Maintenance Procedures - Keep it running

docs.title: Important Notes
docs.text:
Production Use

This documentation uses example.com as a placeholder domain. Replace with your actual domain in production environments.

Data Safety

Several operations in this training involve disk partitioning and data destruction. Always verify you're working on the correct system before executing commands.

Documentation Format

Each document follows this pattern: 1. Prerequisites checklist 2. Step-by-step instructions 3. Verification steps 4. Daily completion checklist

docs.title: Support
docs.text: For issues or questions: - Review the Troubleshooting Guide - Check Common Commands - Refer to specific technology documentation

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Ready to begin? Start with Prerequisites to ensure you have everything needed, then proceed to Day 1 - Rescue Mode.

docs.title: Rescue Mode and Initial Access
docs.text: Brief description: Access your Hetzner server in rescue mode to prepare for Proxmox installation.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to activate rescue mode in Hetzner Robot
  • How to SSH into the rescue environment
  • How to identify NVMe storage devices

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] Hetzner EX44 server ordered and delivered
  • [ ] Access to Hetzner Robot portal (https://robot.hetzner.com)
  • [ ] SSH client installed on your local machine
  • [ ] Server IP address from Hetzner

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 30-45 minutes

docs.title: Step 1: Access Hetzner Robot Portal
docs.text:
1. Open your web browser
  2. Navigate to https://robot.hetzner.com
  3. Log in with your credentials
  4. Click on your server in the server list

docs.title: Step 2: Activate Rescue Mode
docs.text:
1. Click the "Rescue" tab in the server details
  2. Select "Linux 64-bit" from the operating system dropdown
  3. Click "Activate rescue mode" button
  4. A root password will be displayed - copy this to a secure location
  5. Click "Send CTRL+ALT+DEL to reset" or power cycle the server
  6. Wait 2-3 minutes for the server to boot into rescue mode

docs.title: Step 3: SSH into Rescue Mode
docs.text:
Open your terminal and connect:

ssh root@<your-server-ip>

When prompted for password, enter the rescue password from Step 2.

You should see output similar to: 

Rescue System 6.0.1
root@rescue ~ #

docs.title: Step 4: Verify NVMe Devices
docs.text:
List available storage devices:

lsblk

Expected output: 

NAME        MAJ:MIN RM   SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT
nvme0n1     259:0    0 476.9G  0 disk 
nvme1n1     259:1    0 476.9G  0 disk

Verify device details:

fdisk -l | grep nvme

Expected output: 

Disk /dev/nvme0n1: 476.94 GiB, 512110190592 bytes, 1000215216 sectors
Disk /dev/nvme1n1: 476.94 GiB, 512110190592 bytes, 1000215216 sectors

Device Verification

Ensure you see exactly two NVMe devices (/dev/nvme0n1 and /dev/nvme1n1) before proceeding. If you see different devices, stop and verify your server configuration.

docs.title: Step 5: Update Rescue System
docs.text:
Update package lists:

apt update

Install essential tools:

apt install -y wget curl vim

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Successfully accessed Hetzner Robot portal
  • [ ] Rescue mode activated successfully
  • [ ] SSH connection established to rescue environment
  • [ ] Both NVMe devices identified (/dev/nvme0n1 and /dev/nvme1n1)
  • [ ] Rescue system updated

docs.title: Issue: Cannot connect via SSH
docs.text: Solution: Wait 3-5 minutes after activating rescue mode for the server to fully boot.

docs.title: Issue: Permission denied (password)
docs.text: Solution: Copy the password exactly as shown in Hetzner Robot (case-sensitive). If still failing, reactivate rescue mode to get a new password.

docs.title: Issue: Only one NVMe device visible
docs.text: Solution: Check Hetzner Robot hardware details. Contact Hetzner support if hardware doesn't match order.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Proceed to ZFS Setup to create your storage pool.

docs.title: Proxmox Installation (QEMU Method)
docs.text: Brief description: Install Proxmox VE on your Hetzner server using the QEMU-based ISO installation method from rescue mode.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to boot Proxmox installer in a virtual machine from rescue mode
  • How to pass NVMe drives through to the installer
  • How to access the installer via VNC
  • How to configure Proxmox with ZFS during installation

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] Rescue mode activated and SSH connected
  • [ ] Both NVMe devices identified (/dev/nvme0n1 and /dev/nvme1n1)
  • [ ] VNC client installed on your local machine
  • [ ] At least 4GB free space for ISO download

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text:
45-60 minutes

Data Destruction Warning

This installation will erase all data on your NVMe drives. Ensure you have no important data. This is a fresh server setup.

docs.title: Step 1: Check Boot Mode (UEFI/BIOS)
docs.text:
Determine if your server uses UEFI or legacy BIOS:

[ -d "/sys/firmware/efi" ] && echo "UEFI" || echo "BIOS"

Note the result - you'll need it for the QEMU commands.

docs.title: Step 2: Forward VNC Port
docs.text:
Forward a local port to access the virtual machine's VNC console:

ssh -L 5900:localhost:5900 root@<your-server-ip>

Keep this SSH session open during the entire installation process.

docs.title: Step 3: Download Proxmox VE ISO
docs.text:
Download the Proxmox VE ISO from the official source:

cd /tmp
wget https://enterprise.proxmox.com/iso/proxmox-ve_9.0-1.iso

Verify the download:

ls -lh proxmox-ve_9.0-1.iso

Expected size: ~1.1GB

docs.title: Step 4: Start Proxmox Installer in QEMU
docs.text: Start a virtual machine with the Proxmox ISO and pass your NVMe drives through:

docs.title: UEFI Mode (with OVMF BIOS)
docs.text:
qemu-system-x86_64 \
  -enable-kvm \
  -bios /usr/share/ovmf/OVMF.fd \
  -k en-us \
  -cpu host \
  -smp 4 \
  -m 4096 \
  -boot d \
  -cdrom ./pve.iso \
  -drive file=/dev/nvme0n1,format=raw,media=disk,if=virtio \
  -drive file=/dev/nvme1n1,format=raw,media=disk,if=virtio \
  -vnc :0

docs.title: Legacy BIOS Mode (without OVMF)
docs.text:
qemu-system-x86_64 \
  -enable-kvm \
  -k en-us \
  -cpu host \
  -smp 4 \
  -m 4096 \
  -boot d \
  -cdrom ./pve.iso \
  -drive file=/dev/nvme0n1,format=raw,media=disk,if=virtio \
  -drive file=/dev/nvme1n1,format=raw,media=disk,if=virtio \
  -vnc :0

Drive Naming

If you have SATA drives instead of NVMe, use /dev/sda and /dev/sdb instead of /dev/nvme0n1 and /dev/nvme1n1.

docs.title: Step 5: Connect via VNC
docs.text:
Connect your VNC client to:

127.0.0.1:5900

You should see the Proxmox installer boot menu.

VNC Timeout

The VNC connection may timeout after you press "Install". Simply reconnect to 127.0.0.1:5900 if this happens.

docs.title: Step 6: Complete Proxmox Installation
docs.text:
Follow the on-screen installer steps:

  1. Select "Install Proxmox VE"
  2. Choose your target disks (both NVMe drives should be visible)
  3. Select "ZFS - RAID1" for the filesystem (the installer handles ZFS setup)
  4. Configure:
  5. Location/Timezone
  6. Password
  7. Network (temporary - we'll configure properly later)
  8. Wait for installation to complete

docs.title: Step 7: Stop QEMU and Prepare for Boot
docs.text:
Once installation shows "Finished", stop QEMU by pressing Ctrl+C in your SSH terminal.

Before rebooting the server, you need to identify the correct network interface name.

docs.title: Step 8: Identify Network Interface Name
docs.text:
Run the predict-check command to find the actual interface name:

predict-check

Example output: 

eth0 -> enp0s31f6

In this example, enp0s31f6 is the true interface name.

Check the current network configuration in rescue mode:

netdata

Example output: 

Network data:
   eth0  LINK: yes
         MAC:  aa:bb:cc:dd:cc:ff
         IP:   192.168.1.100/24
         IPv6: (none)
         Intel(R) PRO/1000 Network Driver

Note the MAC address and IP configuration.

docs.title: Step 9: Boot into Proxmox
docs.text: Start Proxmox without the ISO to boot from the installed system:

docs.title: UEFI Mode (with OVMF BIOS)
docs.text:
qemu-system-x86_64 \
  -enable-kvm \
  -bios /usr/share/ovmf/OVMF.fd \
  -cpu host \
  -k en-us \
  -device virtio-net,netdev=net0 \
  -netdev user,id=net0,hostfwd=tcp:127.0.0.1:2222-:22 \
  -smp 4 \
  -m 4096 \
  -drive file=/dev/nvme0n1,format=raw,media=disk,if=virtio \
  -drive file=/dev/nvme1n1,format=raw,media=disk,if=virtio \
  -vnc :0

docs.title: Legacy BIOS Mode (without OVMF)
docs.text:
qemu-system-x86_64 \
  -enable-kvm \
  -cpu host \
  -k en-us \
  -device virtio-net,netdev=net0 \
  -netdev user,id=net0,hostfwd=tcp:127.0.0.1:2222-:22 \
  -smp 4 \
  -m 4096 \
  -drive file=/dev/nvme0n1,format=raw,media=disk,if=virtio \
  -drive file=/dev/nvme1n1,format=raw,media=disk,if=virtio \
  -vnc :0

This command includes network forwarding, allowing SSH access to the VM via ssh -p 2222 root@127.0.0.1.

docs.title: Step 10: Configure Network
docs.text:
Connect via VNC again. Log in to the Proxmox console and edit the network configuration:

nano /etc/network/interfaces

Adjust the interface name to match what you found in Step 8. See Network Configuration for the complete network setup.

docs.title: Step 11: Reboot into Production
docs.text:
Power cycle the server or use the Proxmox console to reboot:

reboot

Your SSH session will disconnect. The server will boot directly into Proxmox.

docs.title: Step 12: Access Proxmox Web UI
docs.text:
After 5 minutes, access the Proxmox Web UI:

https://<your-server-ip>:8006

SSL Certificate Warning

You will see a browser warning about an untrusted certificate. This is normal. Click "Advanced" and proceed.

Login credentials: - Username: root - Password: (set during installation)

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Proxmox VE ISO downloaded successfully
  • [ ] QEMU virtual machine started with NVMe drives passed through
  • [ ] VNC connection established to installer
  • [ ] Proxmox installed on ZFS RAID1
  • [ ] Network interface configured correctly
  • [ ] Web UI accessible on port 8006
  • [ ] Successfully logged in as root

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Proceed to Network Configuration to configure your network setup.

docs.title: Network Configuration
docs.text: Brief description: Configure bridged network setup for Proxmox VE on Hetzner with public and private networks.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How Hetzner's IP/MAC binding works
  • How to configure bridged network setup with public IP
  • How to set up private internal network with NAT

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] Proxmox VE installed and accessible via web UI
  • [ ] Server IP address and gateway from Hetzner
  • [ ] Additional IPs or subnets (if applicable)
  • [ ] SSH access to Proxmox host

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 15-20 minutes

docs.title: Understanding Hetzner Network
docs.text:
Hetzner has strict IP/MAC binding for security. Key points:

  • Main IP and additional single IPs are bound to the server's main MAC address
  • Additional subnets (except failover) can use virtual MAC addresses
  • Failover IPs and failover subnets require routed setup
  • Virtual MAC addresses can only be generated for single additional IPs in Robot Panel

docs.title: Network Configuration
docs.text:
Edit /etc/network/interfaces on the Proxmox host:

nano /etc/network/interfaces

Add the following configuration:

# Loopback
auto lo
iface lo inet loopback
iface lo inet6 loopback

# Physical Interface (replace enp5s0 with your actual interface)
iface enp5s0 inet manual

# Include other network configurations
source /etc/network/interfaces.d/*

# Proxmox Bridge - Public Network
auto vmbr0
iface vmbr0 inet static
      address      YOUR_IP/26
      gateway      YOUR_GATEWAY
      bridge-ports YOUR_INTERFACE
      bridge-stp   off
      bridge-fd    0
      up           sysctl -p

# Proxmox Bridge - Private Internal Network (NAT)
auto vmbr2
iface vmbr2 inet static
      address      192.168.192.5/18
      bridge-ports none
      bridge-stp   off
      bridge-fd    0
      post-up      iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s '192.168.192.0/18' -o vmbr0 -j MASQUERADE
      post-down    iptables -t nat -D POSTROUTING -s '192.168.192.0/18' -o vmbr0 -j MASQUERADE
      post-up      iptables -t raw -I PREROUTING -i fwbr+ -j CT --zone 1
      post-down    iptables -t raw -D PREROUTING -i fwbr+ -j CT --zone 1

docs.title: Configuration Values
docs.text:
Replace the following placeholders with your actual values:

Placeholder Description Example YOUR_IP/26 Your server's public IP with subnet mask 192.168.1.100/26 YOUR_GATEWAY Your server's gateway IP 192.168.1.1 YOUR_INTERFACE Your physical network interface enp5s0

Verify Interface Name

Use ip addr or predict-check to identify your actual network interface name before configuring.

docs.title: vmbr0 - Public Bridge
docs.text:
• Connects to the physical network interface
  • Handles all public traffic
  • VMs attached to this bridge get direct network access
  • Uses the main IP and MAC from Hetzner

docs.title: vmbr2 - Private Internal Network
docs.text:
• Internal-only bridge with no physical port
  • Provides NAT (Network Address Translation) to vmbr0
  • IP range: 192.168.192.0/18 (192.168.192.1 - 192.168.255.254)
  • Use this for internal-only VMs that don't need public IPs

docs.title: Applying Network Changes
docs.text:
After making changes, apply them:

systemctl restart networking

Running VMs

If VMs are running when restarting networking, you must stop and start them again (not restart) to recreate their network connections.

docs.title: Port Forwarding for Nginx Proxy
docs.text: Since all VMs are on vmbr2 (private network), we need to forward ports 80 and 443 from vmbr0 (public) to the nginx-proxy VM.

docs.title: Create Port Forwarding Script
docs.text:
Create a script to set up port forwarding:

sudo nano /etc/network/if-pre-up.d/iptables-nat-forward

Add the following content:

#!/bin/sh
/sbin/iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -i vmbr0 -p tcp --dport 80 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.192.20:80
/sbin/iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -i vmbr0 -p tcp --dport 443 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.192.20:443
/sbin/iptables -A FORWARD -p tcp -d 192.168.192.20 --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
/sbin/iptables -A FORWARD -p tcp -d 192.168.192.20 --dport 443 -j ACCEPT

Make it executable:

sudo chmod +x /etc/network/if-pre-up.d/iptables-nat-forward

Apply the rules immediately:

/etc/network/if-pre-up.d/iptables-nat-forward

Nginx Proxy IP

The nginx-proxy VM should have IP 192.168.192.20. If you use a different IP, update the rules accordingly.

docs.title: For VMs with Public IPs (attach to vmbr0)
docs.text: Configure via Proxmox GUI: 1. Create/edit VM → Hardware → Network Device 2. Select Bridge: vmbr0 3. Add MAC address from Hetzner Robot (for additional IPs)

docs.title: For VMs with Internal IPs only (attach to vmbr2)
docs.text:
# Inside the VM, configure static IP
auto ens18
iface ens18 inet static
        address 192.168.192.100/18
        gateway 192.168.192.5

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Network configuration matches your setup
  • [ ] Proxmox host is reachable via main IP
  • [ ] vmbr0 bridge is active
  • [ ] vmbr2 bridge is active with NAT
  • [ ] VMs can communicate as expected
  • [ ] Internal VMs can reach the internet via NAT

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] Proxmox VE installed and accessible
  • [ ] ZFS storage pool rpool visible in Proxmox
  • [ ] Ubuntu Server 24.04 LTS ISO uploaded (see below)
  • [ ] Network bridges vmbr0 and vmbr2 configured (see Network Configuration)

docs.title: Upload Ubuntu ISO
docs.text:
Before creating VMs, upload the Ubuntu ISO:

  1. Download Ubuntu Server 24.04 LTS: 

    # From your local machine
wget https://releases.ubuntu.com/24.04/ubuntu-24.04-live-server-amd64.iso

  2. In Proxmox Web UI:

  3. Navigate to Datacenter → Storage → local
  4. Click ISO Images
  5. Click Upload
  6. Select the downloaded ISO file

  7. Wait for upload to complete

  8. Verify upload:

  9. ISO should appear in the ISO Images list
  10. Size should be approximately 2GB

docs.title: Recommended Order
docs.text:
1. VM-103 (Nginx Proxy) - Create first for early testing
  2. VM-101 (Rocket.Chat) - Core communication service
  3. VM-102 (Nextcloud) - File sharing service
  4. VM-106 (Keycloak) - Identity management
  5. VM-105 (OpenLDAP) - Directory services
  6. VM-104 (MkDocs) - Documentation (can be created anytime)

docs.title: Alternative: Create All at Once
docs.text: You can also create all VMs in one session, then install Ubuntu on each sequentially.

docs.title: Verification Checklist
docs.text:
Before proceeding to individual VM creation:

  • [ ] Ubuntu 24.04 LTS ISO uploaded to Proxmox
  • [ ] IP address plan documented
  • [ ] Sufficient storage space in ZFS pool (minimum 130GB total)
  • [ ] Network bridges vmbr0 and vmbr2 configured
  • [ ] Proxmox Web UI accessible

docs.title: Time Estimate
docs.text:
• Planning: 15-30 minutes
  • Creating all VMs: 2-3 hours
  • Installing Ubuntu on each: 2-3 hours
  • Total Day 2: 4-5 hours

docs.title: Issue: Not enough storage space
docs.text:
Solution: Check ZFS pool capacity: 

zpool list

docs.title: Issue: Cannot upload ISO
docs.text: Solution: - Verify storage location has enough space - Try alternative upload method via command line - Check browser console for errors

docs.title: Issue: Network bridge not configured
docs.text: Solution: - Check network configuration in Datacenter → Node → Network - Ensure both vmbr0 (public) and vmbr2 (private/NAT) are configured - See Network Configuration for setup details

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Choose your first VM to create: - Rocket.Chat VM - VM-101 - Nextcloud VM - VM-102 - Nginx Proxy VM - VM-103 - MkDocs VM - VM-104 - LDAP VM - VM-105 - Keycloak VM - VM-106

Or create all VMs following the same pattern for each.

docs.title: Rocket.Chat VM Creation (VM-101)
docs.text: Brief description: Create VM-101 for Rocket.Chat with 2 vCPU, 4GB RAM, and 30GB storage.

docs.title: VM Specifications
docs.text:
• VM ID: 101
  • Name: rocketchat
  • CPU: 2 vCPU
  • RAM: 4 GiB
  • Disk: 30 GiB (ZFS)
  • OS: Ubuntu Server 24.04 LTS
  • Network: vmbr2 (private internal network)
  • Static IP: 192.168.192.101/18
  • Gateway: 192.168.192.5
  • Purpose: Rocket.Chat server

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] Proxmox Web UI accessible
  • [ ] Ubuntu 24.04 LTS ISO uploaded
  • [ ] ZFS storage pool available
  • [ ] IP address planned from overview

docs.title: Step 1: Create VM in Proxmox
docs.text:
1. Log into Proxmox Web UI at https://<proxmox-ip>:8006
  2. Click on your node in the left sidebar

docs.text:
If you want to separate Nextcloud data:

  1. In Proxmox, add additional disk to VM 102:
  2. Hardware → Add → Hard Disk
  3. Storage: rpool
  4. Size: 100GB or more

  5. Format: qcow2

  6. In VM, format and mount: 

    # Find new disk (likely /dev/sdb)
lsblk

# Format
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb

# Create mount point
sudo mkdir /mnt/nextcloud-data

# Mount
sudo mount /dev/sdb /mnt/nextcloud-data

# Add to fstab
echo '/dev/sdb /mnt/nextcloud-data ext4 defaults 0 2' | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab

docs.title: Enable Swap (Optional)
docs.text:

# Create 2GB swap file
sudo fallocate -l 2G /swapfile
sudo chmod 600 /swapfile
sudo mkswap /swapfile
sudo swapon /swapfile

# Add to fstab
echo '/swapfile none swap sw 0 0' | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab

If you selected Rocket.Chat during Ubuntu installation, the snap is already installed. Skip Step 2 if already done.

docs.title: Step 1: SSH to Rocket.Chat VM
docs.text: ssh admin@192.168.192.101

docs.title: Step 2: Verify Rocket.Chat Snap
docs.text:
Check if Rocket.Chat snap is installed:

snap list rocketchat

If not installed, install it:

sudo snap install rocketchat

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Rocket.Chat
docs.text:
Enable HTTPS (required for Rocket.Chat):

sudo snap set rocketchat port=3000
sudo snap set rocketchat https=enabled
sudo snap set rocketchat ssl-certs=enabled

Start Rocket.Chat:

sudo snap start rocketchat

Check status:

sudo snap services rocketchat

docs.title: Step 4: Wait for Initial Setup
docs.text:
Rocket.Chat takes a few minutes to initialize on first boot. Wait 2-3 minutes then check:

sudo snap logs rocketchat -n 50

Look for messages indicating the server is ready.

docs.title: Step 5: Access Rocket.Chat
docs.text:
Open your browser and navigate to:

http://192.168.192.101:3000

Or if HTTPS is configured:

https://192.168.192.101

docs.title: Step 6: Initial Admin Setup
docs.text:
1. Follow the on-screen wizard to create the first admin user
  2. Configure your organization details
  3. Complete the setup

docs.title: Step 7: Configure Nginx Reverse Proxy
docs.text:
On the Nginx Proxy VM (192.168.192.20), create a configuration file:

ssh admin@192.168.192.20
sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/rocketchat

Add the following configuration:

upstream rocketchat {
    server 192.168.192.101:3000;
}

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name chat.example.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://rocketchat;
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
        proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forward-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forward-Proto http;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Port $server_port;
    }
}

Enable the site:

sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/rocketchat /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/
sudo nginx -t
sudo systemctl reload nginx

docs.title: Step 8: Update Firewall (if enabled)
docs.text:
On the Rocket.Chat VM, allow traffic from nginx-proxy:

sudo ufw allow from 192.168.192.20 to any port 3000
sudo ufw reload

docs.title: Step 9: Verify Access via Domain
docs.text:
After DNS propagation, access Rocket.Chat via your domain:

https://chat.example.com

docs.title: Common Snap Commands
docs.text:
# Stop Rocket.Chat
sudo snap stop rocketchat

# Start Rocket.Chat
sudo snap start rocketchat

# Restart Rocket.Chat
sudo snap restart rocketchat

# View logs
sudo snap logs rocketchat -f

# Check status
snap services rocketchat

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
To set custom environment variables:

sudo snap set rocketchat root-url=https://chat.example.com
sudo snap restart rocketchat

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Rocket.Chat snap is installed
  • [ ] Service is running
  • [ ] Accessible at http://192.168.192.101:3000
  • [ ] Nginx proxy configured
  • [ ] Accessible via domain (chat.example.com)
  • [ ] Admin account created

docs.title: Issue: Rocket.Chat not starting
docs.text:
Solution: Check logs: 

sudo snap logs rocketchat -n 100

docs.title: Issue: Cannot connect via domain
docs.text: Solution: - Verify DNS points to your server IP - Check nginx configuration: sudo nginx -t - Check firewall on nginx-proxy: sudo ufw status

docs.title: Issue: WebSocket connection errors
docs.text: Solution: Ensure nginx proxy headers include WebSocket support (shown in config above)

docs.title: Nextcloud Installation
docs.text: Brief description: Install Nextcloud on VM-102 using Snap and configure for reverse proxy.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to install Nextcloud via Snap
  • How to configure trusted domains and proxies
  • How to verify the installation

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] VM-102 created with Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
  • [ ] Nextcloud snap selected during Ubuntu installation (or install manually below)
  • [ ] IP address: 192.168.192.102
  • [ ] Nginx Proxy VM (192.168.192.20) installed
  • [ ] Domain: cloud.yourdomain.com

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text:
30-45 minutes

Snap Already Installed

If you selected Nextcloud during Ubuntu installation, the snap is already installed. Skip Steps 2-4 if already done.

docs.title: Step 1: SSH to Nextcloud VM
docs.text: ssh admin@192.168.192.102

docs.title: Step 2: Update System
docs.text: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

docs.title: Step 3: Install Snap (if needed)
docs.text:
sudo apt install -y snapd

Verify:

snap version

docs.title: Step 4: Install Nextcloud
docs.text:
sudo snap install nextcloud

This will take 10-15 minutes. Monitor progress:

sudo snap changes

Wait until status shows "Done".

docs.title: Step 5: Create Admin Account
docs.text:
sudo nextcloud.manual-install admin <strong-password>

Replace <strong-password> with a secure password.

docs.title: Step 6: Configure for Reverse Proxy
docs.text:
Configure trusted domains and trusted proxies:

# Add your domain
sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:set trusted_domains 1 --value=cloud.yourdomain.com

# Add nginx-proxy IP as trusted proxy
sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:set trusted_proxies 0 --value=192.168.192.20

# Set URL overwrite
sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:set overwrite.cli.url --value=https://cloud.yourdomain.com/

# Set protocol overwrite
sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:set overwriteprotocol --value=https

Restart Nextcloud:

sudo snap restart nextcloud

Verify configuration:

sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:get trusted_domains
sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:get trusted_proxies

docs.title: Step 7: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Check Nextcloud status:

sudo nextcloud.occ status

Expected output: 

  - installed: true
  - version: 28.0.x.x
  - versionstring: 28.0.x
  - edition:

docs.title: Step 8: Configure Background Jobs
docs.text:
Set cron for background tasks:

sudo nextcloud.occ background:cron

Add cron job:

echo "*/5 * * * * root /snap/bin/nextcloud.occ background:cron" | sudo tee /etc/cron.d/nextcloud

docs.title: Step 9: Configure Memory Caching
docs.text:
Enable APCu for better performance:

sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:set memcache.local --value='\OC\Memcache\APCu'

docs.title: Step 10: Adjust PHP Memory Limit
docs.text: sudo snap set nextcloud php.memory-limit=512M

docs.title: Step 11: Test Access
docs.text:
After configuring Nginx reverse proxy:

  1. Open browser: https://cloud.yourdomain.com

  2. Login with:

  3. Username: admin

  4. Password: [password you set in Step 5]

  5. You should see the Nextcloud dashboard

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:

  • [ ] Admin account created
  • [ ] Trusted domains configured
  • [ ] Trusted proxies configured (nginx-proxy IP)
  • [ ] SSL certificates obtained
  • [ ] Access via domain working

docs.title: Issue: "Access through untrusted domain"
docs.text:
Solution: Check trusted_domains configuration: 

sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:get trusted_domains

docs.title: Issue: Infinite redirect loop
docs.text:
Solution: Ensure trusted_proxies is set: 

sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:get trusted_proxies

docs.title: Issue: SSL certificate errors
docs.text:
Solution: Check overwriteprotocol setting: 

sudo nextcloud.occ config:system:get overwriteprotocol

docs.title: Nginx Reverse Proxy and SSL
docs.text: Brief description: Install Nginx, configure reverse proxy for all services, and obtain SSL certificates.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to install Nginx and Certbot
  • How to configure port forwarding on Proxmox host
  • How to configure reverse proxy for backend services
  • How to obtain and configure SSL certificates

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] VM-103 (nginx-proxy) created with IP 192.168.192.20
  • [ ] All backend VMs installed (Rocket.Chat, Nextcloud, etc.)
  • [ ] Domain names pointing to your server IP
  • [ ] Ports 80 and 443 forwarded to nginx-proxy VM

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 1-2 hours

docs.title: Step 1: Install Nginx and Certbot
docs.text:
SSH to nginx-proxy VM:

ssh admin@192.168.192.20

Update and install:

sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
sudo apt install nginx certbot python3-certbot-nginx -y
sudo systemctl enable --now nginx

Verify:

nginx -v

Test:

curl http://localhost

Should show "Welcome to nginx!"

docs.title: Step 2: Port Forwarding on Proxmox Host
docs.text:
On the Proxmox host (SSH in), set up port forwarding so internet traffic reaches the nginx-proxy VM:

# Forward public 80 → Nginx VM port 80
iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -i vmbr0 -p tcp --dport 80  -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.192.20:80
iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -i vmbr0 -p tcp --dport 443 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.192.20:443

# Allow the forwarded traffic
iptables -A FORWARD -p tcp -d 192.168.192.20 --dport 80  -j ACCEPT
iptables -A FORWARD -p tcp -d 192.168.192.20 --dport 443 -j ACCEPT

Make persistent (create script):

sudo nano /etc/network/if-pre-up.d/iptables-nat-forward

Add the 4 iptables lines above, save, then:

sudo chmod +x /etc/network/if-pre-up.d/iptables-nat-forward

Test from remote computer:

curl http://<your-public-ip>

Should show the default Nginx welcome page.

docs.title: Step 3: DNS Setup
docs.text:
You need real domains/subdomains pointed to your server IP. Examples:

Domain A Record nextcloud.yourdomain.com YOUR_PUBLIC_IP chat.yourdomain.com YOUR_PUBLIC_IP auth.yourdomain.com YOUR_PUBLIC_IP docs.yourdomain.com YOUR_PUBLIC_IP

DNS Required for Let's Encrypt

Without domains pointing to your server, Let's Encrypt won't work (HTTP-01 challenge needs public port 80 open and domain resolving correctly).

Use dig or online DNS checker to verify DNS propagation.

docs.title: Step 4: Obtain Let's Encrypt Certificates
docs.text:
Run Certbot to get certificates:

sudo certbot --nginx -d nextcloud.yourdomain.com -d chat.yourdomain.com

Follow prompts: - Enter email (for renewal reminders) - Agree to terms of service - Choose redirect (recommended: yes → auto-redirect HTTP to HTTPS)

Certbot will create/update /etc/nginx/sites-available/ with SSL configuration.

docs.title: Step 5: Configure Nginx Server Blocks
docs.text: Certbot creates basic configs, but you may need to refine them for proper proxying.

docs.title: Nextcloud Configuration
docs.text:
Edit or create:

sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/nextcloud.yourdomain.com

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name nextcloud.yourdomain.com;
    return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
}

server {
    listen 443 ssl http2;
    server_name nextcloud.yourdomain.com;

    ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/nextcloud.yourdomain.com/fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/nextcloud.yourdomain.com/privkey.pem;
    include /etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-nginx.conf;
    ssl_dhparam /etc/letsencrypt/ssl-dhparams.pem;

    client_max_body_size 10G;
    client_body_timeout 300s;

    # Proxy to Nextcloud VM (snap uses port 80 internally)
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://192.168.192.102:80;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Host $host;
        proxy_read_timeout 3600s;
        proxy_redirect off;
    }

    # Handle .well-known for CalDAV/CardDAV
    location /.well-known/carddav { return 301 $scheme://$host/remote.php/dav; }
    location /.well-known/caldav  { return 301 $scheme://$host/remote.php/dav; }
    location ^~ /.well-known       { return 301 $scheme://$host/index.php$uri; }
}

docs.title: Rocket.Chat Configuration
docs.text:
sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/chat.yourdomain.com

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name chat.yourdomain.com;
    return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
}

server {
    listen 443 ssl http2;
    server_name chat.yourdomain.com;

    ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/chat.yourdomain.com/fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/chat.yourdomain.com/privkey.pem;
    include /etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-nginx.conf;
    ssl_dhparam /etc/letsencrypt/ssl-dhparams.pem;

    # Rocket.Chat runs on port 3000 by default
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://192.168.192.101:3000;
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
        proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        proxy_read_timeout 86400;
        proxy_redirect off;
    }
}

docs.title: Step 6: Enable Configs and Test
docs.text:
sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/nextcloud.yourdomain.com /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/
sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/chat.yourdomain.com /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/

docs.title: Step 7: Configure Backend Firewall (if UFW enabled)
docs.text:
On each backend VM, allow traffic from nginx-proxy:

# On Rocket.Chat VM
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.192.20 to any port 3000

# On Nextcloud VM  
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.192.20 to any port 80

# On Keycloak VM
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.192.20 to any port 8080

# On MkDocs VM
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.192.20 to any port 8000

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Nginx installed and running
  • [ ] Port forwarding working (curl public IP shows nginx page)
  • [ ] DNS pointing to server IP
  • [ ] SSL certificates obtained for all domains
  • [ ] All server blocks enabled
  • [ ] HTTPS access working for all services

docs.title: Issue: curl shows empty response
docs.text: Solution: Check if nginx is running: sudo systemctl status nginx

docs.title: Issue: Certificate validation failed
docs.text:
Solution: Verify DNS is pointing correctly: 

nslookup chat.yourdomain.com

docs.title: Issue: 502 Bad Gateway
docs.text: Solution: - Check backend service is running - Check firewall allows traffic from nginx-proxy - Verify backend IP is correct

docs.title: Issue: WebSocket errors in Rocket.Chat
docs.text:
Solution: Ensure these headers are in config: 

proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";

docs.title: Keycloak Installation and OIDC Configuration
docs.text: Brief description: Install Keycloak 24.0.0, configure OpenLDAP federation, and set up OIDC for SSO.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to install Keycloak 24.0.0
  • How to configure OpenLDAP user federation
  • How to create OIDC clients for SSO
  • How to integrate with Rocket.Chat and Nextcloud

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] VM-106 created and Ubuntu 24.04 LTS installed
  • [ ] VM-105 (OpenLDAP) created and accessible
  • [ ] OpenJDK 17 installed on Keycloak VM
  • [ ] IP addresses documented
  • [ ] Nginx reverse proxy configured

docs.title: Estimated Time
docs.text: 6-8 hours (Days 7-8 combined)

docs.title: Step 1: SSH to Keycloak VM
docs.text: ssh admin@<keycloak-ip>

docs.title: Step 2: Verify Java Installation
docs.text:
java -version

Should show OpenJDK 17.

If not installed:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y openjdk-17-jdk

docs.title: Step 3: Download and Install Keycloak
docs.text:
cd /tmp
wget https://github.com/keycloak/keycloak/releases/download/24.0.0/keycloak-24.0.0.tar.gz

Extract:

sudo tar -xzf keycloak-24.0.0.tar.gz -C /opt/
sudo mv /opt/keycloak-24.0.0 /opt/keycloak

docs.title: Step 4: Create Keycloak User
docs.text:
sudo groupadd keycloak
sudo useradd -r -g keycloak -d /opt/keycloak -s /sbin/nologin keycloak

Set ownership:

sudo chown -R keycloak:keycloak /opt/keycloak
sudo mkdir -p /opt/keycloak/data
sudo mkdir -p /var/log/keycloak
sudo chown keycloak:keycloak /opt/keycloak/data /var/log/keycloak

docs.title: Step 5: Create Systemd Service
docs.text:
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/keycloak.service

Add:

[Unit]
Description=Keycloak Identity Provider
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=idle
User=keycloak
Group=keycloak
ExecStart=/opt/keycloak/bin/kc.sh start --hostname=auth.example.com --http-port=8080 --proxy-headers=xforwarded --log=console
WorkingDirectory=/opt/keycloak
Restart=always
RestartSec=10
Environment="JAVA_OPTS=-Xms1024m -Xmx2048m -Djava.security.egd=file:/dev/./urandom"

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

docs.title: Step 6: Create Admin User
docs.text:
cd /opt/keycloak
sudo -u keycloak bin/kc.sh bootstrap-admin user --username admin --password <admin-password>

Replace <admin-password> with a strong password.

docs.title: Step 7: Start Keycloak
docs.text:
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable keycloak
sudo systemctl start keycloak

Check status:

sudo systemctl status keycloak

View logs:

sudo journalctl -u keycloak -f

Wait for "Listening on: http://0.0.0.0:8080"

Press Ctrl+C to exit logs.

docs.title: Step 8: Access Admin Console
docs.text:
Open browser: https://auth.example.com/admin

Login: - Username: admin - Password: [password you set]

docs.title: Step 9: Configure OpenLDAP (VM-105)
docs.text:
SSH to OpenLDAP VM:

ssh admin@<openldap-ip>

Install OpenLDAP:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y slapd ldap-utils

Reconfigure:

sudo dpkg-reconfigure slapd

Answer wizard: - Omit OpenLDAP server configuration? No - DNS domain name: example.com - Organization name: Your Organization - Administrator password: [strong password] - Database backend: MDB - Remove database when slapd is purged? No - Move old database? Yes

docs.title: Step 10: Configure LDAP TLS
docs.text:
Create certificate directory:

sudo mkdir -p /etc/ldap/certs

Generate self-signed certificate:

cd /etc/ldap/certs
sudo openssl req -new -x509 -days 365 -nodes \
  -out ldap.crt \
  -keyout ldap.key \
  -subj "/C=US/ST=State/L=City/O=Organization/CN=ldap.example.com"

Set permissions:

sudo chown openldap:openldap /etc/ldap/certs/ldap.key
sudo chmod 600 /etc/ldap/certs/ldap.key

docs.title: Step 11: Create Base Structure
docs.text:
Create LDIF file:

```bash cat > ~/base-structure.ldif << 'EOF' dn: ou=users,dc=example,dc=com objectClass: organizationalUnit ou: users

dn: ou=groups,dc=example,dc=com objectClass: organizationalUnit ou: groups

docs.title: Firewall Configuration
docs.text: Brief description: Configure UFW firewall on all VMs with appropriate rules for each service.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to configure UFW firewall
  • How to set up firewall rules for each VM role
  • How to prevent SSH lockout

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] All VMs created and Ubuntu installed
  • [ ] IP addresses documented
  • [ ] SSH access to all VMs confirmed

docs.title: VM Firewall Rules Overview
docs.text: VM Incoming Allowed From rocketchat 3000 nginx-proxy nextcloud 80, 443 nginx-proxy nginx-proxy 80, 443 Internet mkdocs 8000 nginx-proxy openldap 389, 636 keycloak keycloak 8080 nginx-proxy ALL VMs 22 Admin IPs only

docs.title: Rocket.Chat VM (VM-101)
docs.text:
SSH to VM:

ssh admin@<rocketchat-ip>

Configure UFW:

# Install UFW
sudo apt install -y ufw

# Default deny
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing

# Allow SSH (be careful!)
sudo ufw allow from <your-admin-ip> to any port 22

# Allow Rocket.Chat from Nginx Proxy only
sudo ufw allow from <nginx-ip> to any port 3000

# Enable firewall
sudo ufw enable

# Check status
sudo ufw status verbose

docs.title: Nextcloud VM (VM-102)
docs.text:
ssh admin@<nextcloud-ip>

sudo apt install -y ufw
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing
sudo ufw allow from <your-admin-ip> to any port 22
sudo ufw allow from <nginx-ip> to any port 80
sudo ufw enable
sudo ufw status verbose

docs.title: Nginx Proxy VM (VM-103)
docs.text:
ssh admin@<nginx-ip>

sudo apt install -y ufw
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing
sudo ufw allow from <your-admin-ip> to any port 22

# Allow HTTP/HTTPS from anywhere
sudo ufw allow 80/tcp
sudo ufw allow 443/tcp

sudo ufw enable
sudo ufw status verbose

docs.title: MkDocs VM (VM-104)
docs.text:
ssh admin@<mkdocs-ip>

sudo apt install -y ufw
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing
sudo ufw allow from <your-admin-ip> to any port 22
sudo ufw allow from <nginx-ip> to any port 8000
sudo ufw enable
sudo ufw status verbose

docs.title: OpenLDAP VM (VM-105)
docs.text:
ssh admin@<ldap-ip>

sudo apt install -y ufw
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing
sudo ufw allow from <your-admin-ip> to any port 22

# LDAP ports - only from Keycloak
sudo ufw allow from <keycloak-ip> to any port 389
sudo ufw allow from <keycloak-ip> to any port 636

sudo ufw enable
sudo ufw status verbose

docs.title: Keycloak VM (VM-106)
docs.text:
ssh admin@<keycloak-ip>

sudo apt install -y ufw
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing
sudo ufw allow from <your-admin-ip> to any port 22
sudo ufw allow from <nginx-ip> to any port 8080
sudo ufw enable
sudo ufw status verbose

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] UFW installed on all VMs
  • [ ] Default deny incoming configured
  • [ ] SSH allowed from admin IP only
  • [ ] Service ports allowed from appropriate sources
  • [ ] Firewall enabled on all VMs
  • [ ] Status verified on all VMs

docs.title: Issue: Locked out of SSH
docs.text:
Solution: Access via Proxmox console and disable UFW: 

sudo ufw disable

docs.title: Issue: Services not reachable
docs.text:
Solution: Check UFW logs: 

sudo ufw logging on
sudo tail -f /var/log/ufw.log

docs.title: Fail2Ban Configuration
docs.text: Brief description: Install and configure Fail2Ban on all VMs for intrusion prevention.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] Firewall configured on all VMs
  • [ ] SSH access confirmed

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
On each VM:

sudo apt install -y fail2ban
sudo systemctl enable fail2ban
sudo systemctl start fail2ban

docs.title: Basic SSH Protection
docs.text:
Create jail configuration:

sudo nano /etc/fail2ban/jail.local

Add:

[DEFAULT]
bantime = 3600
findtime = 600
maxretry = 3
backend = systemd

[sshd]
enabled = true
port = 22
filter = sshd
logpath = /var/log/auth.log
maxretry = 3

Restart:

sudo systemctl restart fail2ban

docs.title: Nginx Proxy Additional Rules
docs.text:
sudo nano /etc/fail2ban/jail.local

Add:

[nginx-http-auth]
enabled = true
port = http,https
filter = nginx-http-auth
logpath = /var/log/nginx/error.log
maxretry = 3

[nginx-badbots]
enabled = true
port = http,https
filter = nginx-badbots
logpath = /var/log/nginx/access.log
maxretry = 2

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Fail2Ban installed on all VMs
  • [ ] SSH jail enabled
  • [ ] Nginx jails enabled (on proxy VM)
  • [ ] Service running
  • [ ] Status checked with: sudo fail2ban-client status

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: • Backup and Snapshots

docs.title: Backup and ZFS Snapshots
docs.text: Brief description: Configure ZFS snapshots and remote backups for all VMs.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] Proxmox host access
  • [ ] ZFS pool healthy
  • [ ] Storage box or remote server for backups

docs.title: Step 1: Install Sanoid
docs.text:
On Proxmox host:

apt install -y sanoid

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Snapshot Policy
docs.text:
nano /etc/sanoid/sanoid.conf

Add:

[rpool/vm-101-disk-0]
use_template = production

[rpool/vm-102-disk-0]
use_template = production

[rpool/vm-103-disk-0]
use_template = production

[rpool/vm-104-disk-0]
use_template = production

[rpool/vm-105-disk-0]
use_template = production

[rpool/vm-106-disk-0]
use_template = production

[template_production]
frequently = 0
hourly = 24
daily = 7
weekly = 4
monthly = 3
yearly = 0
autosnap = yes
autoprune = yes

docs.title: Step 3: Enable Sanoid
docs.text:
systemctl enable sanoid.timer
systemctl start sanoid.timer

docs.title: Step 4: Create Backup Script
docs.text:
cat > /root/backup-replication.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
STORAGE_BOX="u12345@u12345.your-storagebox.de"
BACKUP_PATH="/backup/proxmox"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)

mkdir -p /tmp/zfs-backup-$DATE

for vm in 101 102 103 104 105 106; do
    echo "Backing up VM $vm..."
    zfs send rpool/vm-${vm}-disk-0@autosnap_$(date +%Y-%m-%d) | \
    gzip > /tmp/zfs-backup-$DATE/vm-${vm}.gz
    scp /tmp/zfs-backup-$DATE/vm-${vm}.gz ${STORAGE_BOX}:${BACKUP_PATH}/
done

rm -rf /tmp/zfs-backup-$DATE
echo "Backup completed: $DATE"
EOF

chmod +x /root/backup-replication.sh

docs.title: Step 5: Schedule Weekly Backups
docs.text:
crontab -e

Add:

0 2 * * 0 /root/backup-replication.sh >> /var/log/backup.log 2>&1

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Sanoid installed
  • [ ] Snapshot policy configured
  • [ ] Timer enabled
  • [ ] Backup script created
  • [ ] Weekly backup scheduled
  • [ ] Test backup completed

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: • CIS Hardening

docs.title: CIS Benchmark Hardening
docs.text: Brief description: Apply CIS benchmark security hardening to Proxmox host and all VMs.

docs.title: SSH Hardening
docs.text:
nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config

Set:

PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
PasswordAuthentication no
MaxAuthTries 3
ClientAliveInterval 300
ClientAliveCountMax 0
Protocol 2
X11Forwarding no

Restart:

systemctl restart sshd

docs.title: Password Policy
docs.text:
apt install -y libpam-pwquality

nano /etc/security/pwquality.conf

Set:

minlen = 14
minclass = 4
maxrepeat = 2
dcredit = -1
ucredit = -1
ocredit = -1
lcredit = -1

docs.title: Kernel Parameters
docs.text:
nano /etc/sysctl.conf

Add:

net.ipv4.ip_forward = 0
net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1
net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1
net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1
kernel.randomize_va_space = 2

Apply:

sysctl -p

docs.title: Auditd
docs.text:
apt install -y auditd audispd-plugins
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

docs.title: VM Hardening Script
docs.text:
Apply to all VMs:

cat > /root/harden-vm.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
VM_IP=$1

ssh admin@$VM_IP << 'REMOTE'
sudo apt install -y libpam-pwquality ufw fail2ban

# SSH hardening
sudo sed -i 's/#PermitRootLogin yes/PermitRootLogin no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sudo sed -i 's/#PasswordAuthentication yes/PasswordAuthentication no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sudo systemctl restart sshd

# Password policy
echo "minlen = 14
minclass = 3
dcredit = -1
ucredit = -1
lcredit = -1
ocredit = -1" | sudo tee /etc/security/pwquality.conf

# Enable UFW
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing
sudo ufw allow 22/tcp
sudo ufw enable

# Enable fail2ban
sudo systemctl enable fail2ban
sudo systemctl start fail2ban

# Secure permissions
sudo chmod 600 /etc/shadow
sudo chmod 600 /etc/gshadow

REMOTE
EOF

chmod +x /root/harden-vm.sh

Run for each VM:

for ip in <rocketchat-ip> <nextcloud-ip> <nginx-ip> <mkdocs-ip> <ldap-ip> <keycloak-ip>; do
    /root/harden-vm.sh $ip
done

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] SSH hardened on all systems
  • [ ] Password policy enforced
  • [ ] Kernel parameters set
  • [ ] Auditd installed and running
  • [ ] VMs hardened with script
  • [ ] Permissions secured

docs.text: • Prometheus & Grafana

docs.title: Prometheus and Grafana Monitoring
docs.text: Brief description: Deploy Prometheus and Grafana monitoring stack on Proxmox host.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• [ ] Proxmox host access
  • [ ] All VMs running
  • [ ] IP addresses documented

docs.title: Step 1: Create User
docs.text: useradd --no-create-home --shell /bin/false prometheus

docs.title: Step 2: Install Prometheus
docs.text:
cd /tmp
wget https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/releases/download/v2.51.0/prometheus-2.51.0.linux-amd64.tar.gz
tar -xzf prometheus-2.51.0.linux-amd64.tar.gz

sudo cp prometheus-2.51.0.linux-amd64/prometheus /usr/local/bin/
sudo cp prometheus-2.51.0.linux-amd64/promtool /usr/local/bin/
sudo cp -r prometheus-2.51.0.linux-amd64/consoles /etc/prometheus
sudo cp -r prometheus-2.51.0.linux-amd64/console_libraries /etc/prometheus

sudo chown -R prometheus:prometheus /etc/prometheus

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Prometheus
docs.text:
sudo nano /etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml

Add:

global:
  scrape_interval: 15s

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'prometheus'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:9090']

  - job_name: 'rocketchat-node'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['<rocketchat-ip>:9100']

  - job_name: 'nextcloud-node'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['<nextcloud-ip>:9100']

  - job_name: 'nginx-node'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['<nginx-ip>:9100']

docs.title: Step 4: Create Service
docs.text:
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/prometheus.service

Add:

[Unit]
Description=Prometheus
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target

[Service]
User=prometheus
Group=prometheus
Type=simple
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/prometheus \
    --config.file /etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml \
    --storage.tsdb.path /var/lib/prometheus/

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Start:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable prometheus
sudo systemctl start prometheus

docs.title: Node Exporter Installation
docs.text:
On each VM:

cd /tmp
wget https://github.com/prometheus/node_exporter/releases/download/v1.7.0/node_exporter-1.7.0.linux-amd64.tar.gz
tar -xzf node_exporter-1.7.0.linux-amd64.tar.gz
sudo cp node_exporter-1.7.0.linux-amd64/node_exporter /usr/local/bin/

# Create service
cat > /tmp/node_exporter.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Node Exporter

[Service]
User=node_exporter
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/node_exporter

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
EOF

sudo useradd --no-create-home --shell /bin/false node_exporter
sudo cp /tmp/node_exporter.service /etc/systemd/system/
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable node_exporter
sudo systemctl start node_exporter

# Allow from Proxmox
sudo ufw allow from <proxmox-ip> to any port 9100

docs.title: Grafana Installation
docs.text:
apt install -y apt-transport-https software-properties-common wget

wget -q -O /usr/share/keyrings/grafana.key https://apt.grafana.com/gpg.key
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/grafana.key] https://apt.grafana.com stable main" | tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/grafana.list

apt update
apt install -y grafana

systemctl enable grafana-server
systemctl start grafana-server

docs.title: Emergency Rollback Procedures
docs.text: Brief description: Procedures for emergency recovery and rollback.

docs.title: VM Rollback
docs.text:
From Proxmox:

  1. Select VM
  2. Go to Snapshots
  3. Select snapshot to restore
  4. Click Rollback
  5. Confirm

docs.title: ZFS Rollback
docs.text:
# List snapshots
zfs list -t snapshot

# Rollback to specific snapshot
zfs rollback rpool/vm-101-disk-0@snapshot-name

docs.title: Rocket.Chat
docs.text:
ssh admin@192.168.192.101
sudo snap restart rocketchat

docs.title: Nextcloud
docs.text: sudo snap restart nextcloud

docs.text: sudo systemctl restart keycloak

docs.title: Full System Recovery
docs.text:
1. Boot from rescue mode
  2. Restore from backup: 
    gunzip -c vm-101-backup.gz | zfs receive rpool/vm-101-disk-0

git clone https://github.com/dmaljkovic/proxmox-lab.git
